rulings.Case Title: IN RE: THE JAMES B. MCCLATCHY TRUST DATED NOVEMBER 15, 1974
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion Of Respondent Co-Trustees To Strike Or Remove Privileged Document From Court Record And To Compel Petitioner To Return Destroy
rulings.Rulings: The Co-Trustees Motion to Strike or Remove Privileged Document is denied in part and granted in part. This case has been assigned to Department 306 for all purposes. Judge Ulmer stayed all proceedings in the case pending appeal except for "administrative" matters that the probate court would have discretion to hear. The administrative matters before the court at the time related to administration of the trust (seeking accountings and information concerning the trust). This matter does not fall within that exception, is not similar to the then-pending motions, and does not relate to administration of the trust. It does relate to a matter that Carlos McClatchy insists is the subject of the appeal (his challenge to the Court's determination of privilege), and removal or striking of the document from the files would interfere with the appellate court's consideration of the issues before it. The request to remove or strike, therefore, is also stayed by the notice of appeal. Moreover, the request amounts to a belated request to modify or reconsider the Court's October 19, 2016 order that provided an express exception for "copies that are attached as exhibits to pleadings not filed under seal." Co-Trustees should have acted long before now if they wished to challenge that order or to remove this exhibit from the file. The request to remove or strike the document from the file is denied. All the same reasoning arguably could apply equally to the alternative request to seal the document. The Court recognizes, however, that leaving a document determined to be privileged in the public record is problematic and that the determination of privilege also establishes a lack of public interest in maintaining access to it. Moreover, no party is prejudiced by sealing the document, and it will remain available for review by the court of appeal. Nor would any purpose be served by referring the matter back to Department 306. Therefore, the Court will order that the "slideshow" document submitted as Exhibit T to the Mosely declaration in support of the motion for summary judgment heard by this Court on January 20, 2016 be sealed. The Co-Trustees are ordered to submit a hard-copy of the entire document entitled "Exhibits M-V to the Declaration of Mark L. Mosley" (filed January 7, 2016) to the probate clerk. The Co-Trustees must leave an "Exhibit T" slip sheet in the hard-copy version of this document, but following that slip sheet, they are to replace the privileged document with a single page stating, "This document has been sealed by order of the court." The probate clerk will then deactivate public access to the original filing of January 7, 2016 and backdate the new entry (containing the sealed Exhibit T) to that date. Given that the Co-Trustees have brought this motion and that they have done so years after the original filing and almost two years after the determination of privilege, it is fair to require them to bear the burden of correcting the record. They must do so promptly so as not to interfere with Carlos McClatchy's ability to file timely the record on appeal. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. Wednesday, August 22, 2018.
